content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

November 2, 2015

Jeffrey A. Mitchell
Kronick Moskovitz Tiedemann & Girard
West Sacramento City Attorney
400 Capitol mall, 27th Floor
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-15-208

Dear Mr. Mitchell:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of West Sacramento City Manager Martin Tuttle regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Moreover, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTIONS

(1) May Mr. Tuttle, as Executive Director of the West Sacramento Redevelopment Successor Agency ("Successor Agency"), participate in decisions regarding an option to purchase and the subsequent sale of property owned by the Successor Agency to the Sacramento-Yolo Port District (the "Port") where he owns a home located within 500 feet of the property which is proposed for future development (the "Site")?

(2) May Mr. Tuttle, as Chief Executive Officer of the Port participate in implementing and negotiating the terms of the option, its assignment to a developer and the related development agreement?

(3) May Mr. Tuttle, as City Manager for the City of West Sacramento, (i) implement the terms of the option and development agreement and (ii) participate in processing the developer's land use entitlement requests?

1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-15-208
Page No. 2

CONCLUSIONS

(1) – (3). Yes. The financial effect of these decisions/activities on his real property is
indistinguishable from the effect on the public generally.

FACTS

Mr. Tuttle has been City Manager of the City since July 2012. By virtue of his position as
City Manager, Mr. Tuttle also serves as Executive Director of the Successor Agency and Chief
Executive Officer of the Port. The City, the Successor Agency, and the Port are distinct legal
entities, but all are run by City staff. The City Council serves as the Board of the Successor Agency
and four of the five members of the Port's Board are City Council members.

The Site, known as the Stone Lock property, is owned by the Successor Agency. The Site
comprises six parcels that total approximately 215 acres of undeveloped land situated east of
Jefferson Boulevard, on the north and south side of the Barge Canal, and immediately north of
existing residential neighborhoods. The City, Successor Agency and Port have been considering
developing the Site. Although still in the design stages, the project at buildout could have up to
2,500 housing units, 1.5 million square feet of office space, 890,000 square feet of retail and up to
800 hotel rooms.

Mr. Tuttle owns and resides on property located within 500 feet of the boundary of the Site.

In 2007, the West Sacramento Redevelopment Agency (the "Predecessor Agency" or
"Agency") selected the Cordish Company (the "Developer") to purchase and develop the Site.
Between 2007 and 2011, the Predecessor Agency negotiated exclusively with the Developer on the
terms of the sale. On March 29, 2011, the City and the Agency approved a joint resolution directing
the Agency's Executive Director to execute an option agreement with the Port. The Port
subsequently entered into an assignment agreement assigning the option to the Developer. The
option agreement gives the holder of the option the right to purchase the Site under certain
conditions. The assignment agreement establishes additional requirements that must be met before
the Developer can exercise the option. A dispute over the Developer's compliance with the terms of
the option agreement and assignment agreement resulted in the Developer assigning the option back
to the Port. The Port and the City then entered into a development agreement, approval of which is
a condition to the exercise of the option.

Mr. Tuttle has participated in a series of decisions by the Port, the City and the Successor
Agency, including the Successor Agency Oversight Board, at a time when he owned a different
property that was also located within 500 feet of the Site. Under former regulations, the
Commission had previously advised that under the "public generally" rules then in effect,
Mr. Tuttle did not have a conflict of interest. He has since sold that property and purchased his
current home.

The Port expects to begin the process of selecting one or more developers to develop the
Site shortly after its purchase. As the Port's Chief Executive Officer, Mr. Tuttle would typically be
involved in the process of preparing Requests for Proposals, making recommendations to the Port,

File No. A-15-208
Page No. 3

Commission on the selection of developer(s), and negotiating the terms under which the Site would
be sold to the selected developer(s).

The City of West Sacramento has 14,447 residential parcels within its boundaries. You have
provided a map showing the location of all residential real property in the City and highlighting
3,612 properties (25% of all residential properties), including Mr. Tuttle's property, that are located
within ¼ mile (1,320 feet) of the Site.² You have also provided a letter from a real estate broker
who studied the impact of the project on both Mr. Tuttle's property and on the other residential
properties located within ¼ mile of the Site. He states that while at this time it is very difficult to
accurately predict the change in value of these properties that would result directly from
development of the Site, any increase in value would be comparable regardless of actual proximity
to the Site. The broker concludes that the effects of development would not be unique to Mr.
Tuttle's property compared with the effects on the other properties. As part of his analysis, the
broker specifically considered the factors set forth in Regulation 18703(c)(1) through (6).

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest. A conflict of interest may arise only when it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect on the official or his or her immediate family that is distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally. (Section 87103.)

Under recently adopted Regulation 18703, a governmental decision's financial effect on a
public official's financial interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally if the
official establishes that a significant segment of the public is affected and the effect on the official's
financial interest is not unique compared to the effect on the significant segment. A "significant
segment," as applied to the facts here, is at least 25% of all residential property within the City of
West Sacramento.

You state that the City has 14,447 residential parcels within its boundaries and 3,612, or
25% of these are located within ¼ mile of the Site. This constitutes a significant segment.

Regulation 18703(c) provides that a "unique effect" on a public official's financial interest
includes a disproportionate effect on:

"(1) The development potential or use of the official's real
property or on the income producing potential of the official's real
property or business entity.

"(2) An official's business entity or real property resulting from the
proximity of a project that is the subject of a decision.

________________
² In a previous request for advice involving the same development, you indicated that these properties are all
located within the three neighborhoods that are adjacent to the Site: Gateway, Arlington Oaks and State
Streets Westmoreland Oaks. (See Mitchell Advice Letter, No. A-14-124(a).)

File No. A-15-208
Page No. 4

"(3) An official's interests in business entities or real properties resulting from the cumulative effect of the official's multiple interests in similar entities or properties that is substantially greater than the effect on a single interest.

"(4) An official's interest in a business entity or real property resulting from the official's substantially greater business volume or larger real property size when a decision affects all interests by the same or similar rate or percentage.

"(5) A person's income, investments, assets or liabilities, or real property if the person is a source of income or gifts to the official.

"(6) An official's personal finances or those of his or her immediate family."

The real estate broker who analyzed the effects of the development on properties located within ¼ mile of the Site stated that, after considering these factors, he did not believe that the effects would be unique to Mr. Tuttle's property. We agree. The properties in the significant segment are all located within the three neighborhoods that adjoin the Site and their proximity to the Site is just ¼ mile. There is no reason to believe that the subject decisions will disproportionately affect a single residence within this area as compared to the other residences within the area. Notwithstanding the proximity of Mr. Tuttle's residence to the Site, we find that any effects on his property would be indistinguishable from the effects on the public generally. Therefore, Mr. Tuttle is not prohibited by the Act's conflict of interest provisions from taking part in the decisions and activities you have described.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: Valentina Joyce
Counsel, Legal Division

VJ:jgl
